paper_map.title: Effects of Mn Contents on the Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of the Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C Alloy
paper_map.abstract_summary: The as-cast Fe–10Al–(5–40)Mn–1.0C (mass%) sheet alloys exhibit a duplex microstructure of austenite (γ) containing κ and κ′ carbides and ferrite (α) with D03 and κ″ phases. The ferrite fraction decreases with increasing Mn content, becoming fully austenitic at 40% Mn. Mechanical properties (UTS 646–887 MPa, elongation 16.3–29.2%, hardness HRC 31–44, damping ratio 0.0603–0.0417, Young's modulus 139–165 GPa) were systematically characterized, with maximum strength observed near 15% Mn and a linear hardness–UTS relationship established. The results provide new data on the microstructural and property evolution of this alloy system produced by sheet casting.
paper_map.research_objective: To investigate the influence of manganese content (5–40 mass%) on the phase constitution, precipitate formation, and mechanical/damping properties of Fe–10Al–1.0C alloy sheet castings.
main_process_variables: air induction melting under N2 atmosphere
as-cast condition without subsequent heat treatment or deformation
tensile test at room temperature, initial strain rate 3.5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹, gauge 25 mm, cross-section 6.25×2.0 mm²
hardness test (Rockwell C scale, ten measurements per alloy)
experimental modal analysis (cantilever beam, half-power bandwidth method) for Young's modulus and damping ratio
microstructural characterization: OM, SEM (fractography), TEM/STEM (200 kV)
key_sections: Introduction: Reviews background on Fe–Al–Mn–C alloys, highlights lack of data for sheet-cast variants with varying Mn, and states the purpose of the study.
Experimental Procedure: Describes alloy melting, sheet casting parameters, chemical composition verification, specimen preparation, and test methods (hardness, tensile, SEM/TEM, modal analysis).

Results and Discussion: Presents optical micrographs showing phase distribution vs. Mn content; TEM analysis of (γ+κ+κ′) and (D03+κ″) regions; tensile properties, hardness, and derived UTS–HRC relation; damping and Young’s modulus data as functions of Mn content.
Conclusions: Summarizes phase evolution, carbide ordering, orientation relationships, and the trends of mechanical/damping properties with Mn content.
